clean_story: How bodies move.  Physics meets living tissue. "Biomechanics" splits into bio (life) and mechanics (forces, motion)—the study of how muscles, bones, and tendons act like levers, springs, and pulleys to create movement. If you’ve ever watched a cheetah’s spine ripple as it sprints—each stretch-and-snap storing energy like a coiled spring—that’s biomechanics in action. Or a kangaroo’s tendon, which recycles 90% of hopping energy, turning legs into pogo sticks. Humans rely on it too:Your Achilles tendon stretches like rubber, returning 35% of the energy your calf muscles spend walking. Without it, every step would demand twice the effort. Engineers borrow these tricks:Prosthetic limbs mimic ankle elasticity. Robots use "passive dynamics" to walk with less power, copying how knees bend naturally when descending stairs. Even plants do it:Venus flytraps snap shut via water pressure shifts—no muscles needed. Three related subjects are animal locomotion, bionic design and soft robotics.
